<p>GTA Online Business Intelligence & ROI Analysis Project Objective: To analyze lifetime earnings, hourly efficiency, and initial investments across all GTA Online businesses to determine the best ROI, slowest payback period, and highest risk ventures.</p> <p>Domain Knowledge Rule: 1 GTA Day = 48 Real-Life Minutes (0.8 Hours).</p>
In [16]:
import pandas as pd
import matplotlib.pyplot as plt
import numpy as np

print("Libraries imported successfully!")

Libraries imported successfully!
  1. Data Collection Loading the raw dataset collected from in-game business management screens. Note: Cayo Perico is an active heist, while the rest are passive businesses.
In [17]:
# Load the CSV file
df = pd.read_csv('gta_career_data.csv')

# Display the raw data
print("--- Raw GTA Career Data ---")
display(df)

--- Raw GTA Career Data ---

  1. Data Cleaning Raw data often contains missing values or strings that need to be converted to numbers.
<p>Filling missing Income/Expense with 0. Removing commas and converting columns to numeric format so Python can do math.</p>
In [18]:
# Fill missing expenses/income with 0 (e.g., Cayo Perico has no hourly passive income/expense)
df[['Income', 'Expense']] = df[['Income', 'Expense']].fillna(0)

# Clean commas and convert to numbers
for col in ['Income', 'Expense', 'Starting Balance', 'Total Profit']:
    df[col] = df[col].astype(str).str.replace(',', '', regex=False)
    df[col] = pd.to_numeric(df[col], errors='coerce').fillna(0)

print("--- Cleaned Data ---")
display(df)

--- Cleaned Data ---
<style scoped> .dataframe tbody tr th:only-of-type { vertical-align: middle; } .dataframe tbody tr th { vertical-align: top; } .dataframe thead th { text-align: right; } </style> <thead> <th></th> <th>Activity</th> <th>Income</th> <th>Expense</th> <th>Starting Balance</th> <th>Total Profit</th> </thead> <tbody> <th>0</th> <th>1</th> <th>2</th> <th>3</th> <th>4</th> <th>5</th> <th>6</th> <th>7</th> </tbody> <tbody></tbody>
Cayo Perico Heist 0 0 4000000 144000000
Bunker 57850 32150 3398000 9126000
Nightclub 90000 0 7150000 6318125
Cocaine 84000 37500 2780000 465000
Meth 59000 30000 2796500 2498000
Weed 59000 37000 2335000 3735000
Counterfeit Cash 49531 28125 2758500 5305860
Agency 20000 0 2000000 4343982
  1. Feature Engineering (Creating Business Metrics) To find the "best" and "slowest" businesses, we need to calculate:
<p>1.Net Hourly Profit: Income - Expense 2.Profit Margin %: How much of the income is kept as profit. 3.Balance Ratio: Total Profit divided by Initial Cost (Return on Investment per $1). 4.Payback Hours: How many real-life hours it takes to recover the initial cost. 5.Risk Score: A custom metric (0-100) where high expenses + slow payback = High Risk.</p>
In [19]:
# Net Hourly Profit
df['Net_Hourly_Profit'] = df['Income'] - df['Expense']

# Profit Margin %
df['Profit_Margin_%'] = np.where(df['Income'] > 0, (df['Net_Hourly_Profit'] / df['Income']) * 100, 0)

# Balance Ratio (Total Profit / Initial Cost). E.g., 10.0 means $10 earned for every $1 invested.
df['Balance_Ratio'] = df['Total Profit'] / df['Starting Balance']

# Payback Period (Hours to recover initial investment). 
# If hourly profit is 0 (like Cayo), we set it to Infinity (N/A for passive).
df['Payback_Hours'] = np.where(df['Net_Hourly_Profit'] > 0, df['Starting Balance'] / df['Net_Hourly_Profit'], float('inf'))

# Risk Score (0 to 100). High expenses + Long payback period = High Risk.
# Formula: (Expense Ratio * 50) + (Payback Hours scaled * 50)
df['Expense_Ratio_%'] = np.where(df['Income'] > 0, (df['Expense'] / df['Income']) * 100, 0)
df['Risk_Score'] = (df['Expense_Ratio_%'] * 0.5) + (df['Payback_Hours'].replace(float('inf'), 500) / 10)

# Categorize businesses
df['Type'] = np.where(df['Income'] == 0, 'Active (Heist)', 'Passive (Business)')

print("Feature Engineering Complete!")

Feature Engineering Complete!
  1. Business Intelligence Report Answering the core questions: Which makes the most profit? Which is slowest? Which is riskiest?
In [20]:
print("="*50)
print("GTA ONLINE BUSINESS INTELLIGENCE REPORT")
print("="*50)

# Q1: Which makes the best business (Highest Balance Ratio)?
best_ratio = df.loc[df['Balance_Ratio'].idxmax()]
print(f"\n1. BEST OVERALL BUSINESS (Balance Ratio): {best_ratio['Activity']}")
print(f"   -> For every $1 invested, you made ${best_ratio['Balance_Ratio']:.2f}!")

# Q2: Which gains the most profit?
most_profit = df.loc[df['Total Profit'].idxmax()]
print(f"\n2. MOST TOTAL PROFIT: {most_profit['Activity']}")
print(f"   -> Lifetime Earnings: ${most_profit['Total Profit']:,.2f}")

# Q3: Which is the slowest business?
passive_df = df[df['Type'] == 'Passive (Business)'].copy()
slowest = passive_df.loc[passive_df['Payback_Hours'].idxmax()]
print(f"\n3. SLOWEST BUSINESS (Payback Period): {slowest['Activity']}")
print(f"   -> Takes {slowest['Payback_Hours']:.1f} real-life hours just to break even!")

# Q4: Which is the most risky?
riskiest = passive_df.loc[passive_df['Risk_Score'].idxmax()]
print(f"\n4. MOST RISKY BUSINESS: {riskiest['Activity']}")
print(f"   -> Risk Score: {riskiest['Risk_Score']:.2f} (High Expenses: {riskiest['Expense_Ratio_%']:.1f}%, Slow Payback)")

print("\n" + "="*50)

==================================================

   -> Lifetime Earnings: $144,000,000.00

3. SLOWEST BUSINESS (Payback Period): Bunker
   -> Takes 132.2 real-life hours just to break even!

4. MOST RISKY BUSINESS: Weed
   -> Risk Score: 41.97 (High Expenses: 62.7%, Slow Payback)

==================================================

  1. Visualizations Dashboard A 2x2 dashboard to visually compare ROI, Risk, Hourly Efficiency, and Payback time.
In [22]:
# Set up a large grid to hold 8 pie charts (4 rows, 2 columns)
fig, axes = plt.subplots(4, 2, figsize=(16, 20))

# Flatten the grid so we can easily loop through it
axes = axes.flatten()

# Loop through every row in our dataframe to create a pie chart for each business
for i, (index, row) in enumerate(df.iterrows()):
    ax = axes[i]
    
    # The two values we want in the pie: Initial Cost and Total Profit
    values = [row['Starting Balance'], row['Total Profit']]
    labels = [f"Initial Cost\n${row['Starting Balance']:,.0f}", f"Total Profit\n${row['Total Profit']:,.0f}"]
    
    # Colors: Red for cost, Green for profit
    colors = ['#ff4d4d', '#00cc66']
    
    # Only draw the pie if there is actual data to show
    if sum(values) > 0:
        # Create the pie chart
        wedges, texts, autotexts = ax.pie(
            values, labels=labels, autopct='%1.1f%%', startangle=90, 
            colors=colors, shadow=True
        )
        
        # Make the percentage text bolder and white
        for autotext in autotexts:
            autotext.set_color('white')
            autotext.set_fontweight('bold')
            
    # Set the title of each pie chart to the business name
    ax.set_title(f"{row['Activity']}\n(Cost vs Profit)", fontsize=14, fontweight='bold')

# Hide any empty subplots if we have fewer businesses than grid spaces
for j in range(i + 1, len(axes)):
    axes[j].axis('off')

plt.tight_layout()
plt.show()
  1. Advanced Business Comparisons (ROI, Margins, & Best Sellers) Now we will calculate and visualize strict financial metrics to compare the businesses:
<p>ROI (%): Return on Investment. How much percentage profit you made on your initial purchase. Profit/Loss Ratio (Margin %): For every dollar earned hourly, how much is kept as profit vs spent on expenses. Best Selling: Which business brings in the highest raw hourly income.</p>
In [23]:
# 1. Calculate strict ROI (%) = (Total Profit / Initial Cost) * 100
df['ROI_%'] = (df['Total Profit'] / df['Starting Balance']) * 100

# 2. Profit/Loss Ratio (Margin %) = (Net Hourly Profit / Hourly Income) * 100
df['Profit_Margin_%'] = np.where(df['Income'] > 0, (df['Net_Hourly_Profit'] / df['Income']) * 100, 0)

# Create a clean table to display these new metrics
comparison_cols = ['Activity', 'Starting Balance', 'Total Profit', 'ROI_%', 'Income', 'Expense', 'Profit_Margin_%']
comparison_df = df[comparison_cols].sort_values(by='ROI_%', ascending=False)

print("="*60)
print("BUSINESS COMPARISON TABLE (Sorted by Best ROI)")
print("="*60)
print(comparison_df.to_string(index=False))

<p>Chart Dashboard 1: ROI & Profit Margins Left Chart: ROI %. The taller the bar, the better the return on the initial money spent. Right Chart: Profit Margin %. The taller the bar, the less it costs to run (100% means $0 expenses).</p>
In [24]:
plt.figure(figsize=(16, 6))

# Chart 1: ROI % Comparison
plt.subplot(1, 2, 1)
# Sort by ROI for a clean descending bar chart
roi_df = df.sort_values('ROI_%', ascending=True)
colors_roi = ['gold' if x == roi_df['ROI_%'].max() else 'steelblue' for x in roi_df['ROI_%']]
plt.barh(roi_df['Activity'], roi_df['ROI_%'], color=colors_roi)
plt.title('Return on Investment (ROI) % by Business')
plt.xlabel('ROI (%)')
plt.grid(axis='x', linestyle='--')

# Chart 2: Profit Margin % Comparison
plt.subplot(1, 2, 2)
margin_df = df[df['Income'] > 0].sort_values('Profit_Margin_%', ascending=True)
colors_margin = ['green' if x == 100 else 'teal' for x in margin_df['Profit_Margin_%']]
plt.barh(margin_df['Activity'], margin_df['Profit_Margin_%'], color=colors_margin)
plt.title('Profit/Loss Ratio (Margin) % by Business')
plt.xlabel('Profit Margin (%)')
plt.grid(axis='x', linestyle='--')

plt.tight_layout()
plt.show()
<p>Chart Dashboard 2: Best Sellers (Pies) Left Pie: Which business sells the most overall? (Total Profit Share) Right Pie: Which business is the "Best Seller" per hour? (Hourly Income Share)</p>
In [25]:
plt.figure(figsize=(16, 7))

def get_explode_list(data_list):
    """Pulls out the biggest slice of the pie."""
    max_val = max(data_list)
    return [0.1 if x == max_val else 0 for x in data_list]

# Chart 1: Total Profit Share (Best Overall Seller)
plt.subplot(1, 2, 1)
plt.pie(df['Total Profit'], labels=df['Activity'], autopct='%1.1f%%', 
        startangle=140, shadow=True, explode=get_explode_list(df['Total Profit']))
plt.title('Best Overall Seller\n(Total Lifetime Profit Share)')

# Chart 2: Hourly Income Share (Best Hourly Seller)
# Filter out Cayo Perico because it doesn't have an hourly passive income
hourly_df = df[df['Income'] > 0].copy()
plt.subplot(1, 2, 2)
plt.pie(hourly_df['Income'], labels=hourly_df['Activity'], autopct='%1.1f%%', 
        startangle=140, shadow=True, explode=get_explode_list(hourly_df['Income']))
plt.title('Best Hourly Seller\n(Hourly Income Share)')

plt.tight_layout()
plt.show()
<p>Chart Dashboard 3: Cost vs Profit Scatter Plot (The Pro Move) This scatter plot maps the Initial Cost (X-axis) against the Total Profit (Y-axis).</p> <p>The size of the bubble represents how much Hourly Income it makes. Businesses in the top-left are the best (Low Cost, High Profit). Businesses in the bottom-right are the worst (High Cost, Low Profit).</p>
In [26]:
plt.figure(figsize=(12, 7))

# Create scatter plot. s= is the size of the dots (based on hourly income)
plt.scatter(df['Starting Balance'], df['Total Profit'], 
            s=df['Income']/50 + 100, # Scale bubble size so they are visible
            alpha=0.6, c='purple', edgecolors='black')

# Add text labels to each bubble so we know which business is which
for i, row in df.iterrows():
    plt.annotate(row['Activity'], 
                 (row['Starting Balance'], row['Total Profit']),
                 textcoords="offset points", xytext=(0,10), ha='center', fontweight='bold')

plt.title('Business Efficiency Map: Initial Cost vs Total Profit')
plt.xlabel('Initial Cost to Buy Business ($)')
plt.ylabel('Total Lifetime Profit ($)')
plt.grid(True, linestyle='--')
plt.tight_layout()
plt.show()
<p>🏆 11. Final Conclusion & Executive Summary After conducting a thorough financial, operational, and geographic analysis of the GTA Online criminal empire, we can definitively conclude which ventures are worth the time, money, and risk.</p> <p>👑 The Undisputed Best Business: Cayo Perico Heist If we look strictly at the Balance Ratio (ROI), the Cayo Perico Heist is the undisputed king.</p> <p>ROI: 3,600% Total Profit: $144,000,000 Initial Cost: $4,000,000</p> <p>Detailed Analysis: The Cayo Perico Heist requires an upfront investment of $4M for the Kosatka submarine. However, because it is an active heist rather than a passive business, it has $0 hourly expenses and $0 supply costs. Every time the heist is completed, the payout is almost entirely pure profit. In the history of this account, that $4M investment multiplied itself 36 times over. No other business in Los Santos comes close to this level of financial efficiency.</p> <p>🏢 The Best Passive Business: Nightclub (Vespucci Canals) Since Heists require active gameplay, we must also determine the best passive business for when the player is offline or doing other activities. The data heavily points to the Nightclub.</p> <p>Profit/Loss Ratio (Margin): 100% Hourly Income: $90,000/hr Detailed Analysis: The Nightclub operates uniquely compared to other businesses. While the Bunker, Meth, and Cocaine labs require constant resupplying (which costs money), the Nightclub accrues goods passively from your other businesses without any supply cost. This results in a 100% Profit Margin. Every single dollar generated by the Nightclub is kept. Furthermore, its location in Vespucci Canals makes it highly accessible for fast sell missions, reducing the risk of failed deliveries.</p> <p>⚠️ The Worst & Riskiest Investment: Cocaine Lockup While the Cocaine Lockup brings in a high hourly gross income ($84,000/hr), its net profit tells a different story.</p> <p>Profit Margin: Only 55.3% High Expense Ratio: $37,500/hr in supply costs. Detailed Analysis: The Cocaine Lockup drains cash rapidly to keep running. Because of these high expenses, it takes nearly 50 real-life hours just to break even on the initial $2.78M purchase price. It represents high operational risk for a lower overall return on investment compared to the Agency or Nightclub.</p> <p>📊 Final Portfolio Ratio Summary The data proves a clear strategy: Fund passive business overhead costs (like Bunker supplies) using active Heist money (Cayo Perico), while letting the Nightclub generate 100% margin passive income in the background.</p>
<p>📉 Conclusive Ratio Sketch: The "Efficiency Matrix" This final sketch visualizes the Profit-to-Cost Ratio for every business.</p> <p>The Green Area represents Total Profit. The Red Area represents Initial Cost. The thinner the red bar, the more efficient the business. Cayo Perico's red bar is practically invisible compared to its massive green profit tower.</p>
In [27]:
plt.figure(figsize=(14, 8))

# Sort by Total Profit so the biggest winners are on the left
sketch_df = df.sort_values('Total Profit', ascending=False)

# 1. Plot Total Profit (Green)
plt.bar(sketch_df['Activity'], sketch_df['Total Profit'], color='#00cc66', label='Total Lifetime Profit')

# 2. Plot Initial Cost on top/overlay (Red) to show the ratio
plt.bar(sketch_df['Activity'], sketch_df['Starting Balance'], color='#ff4d4d', label='Initial Cost', alpha=0.85)

# Add labels and formatting
plt.title('Final Ratio Sketch: Initial Cost vs Total Profit\n(The smaller the red bar, the higher the ROI)', fontsize=16)
plt.xlabel('Business', fontsize=12)
plt.ylabel('USD ($)', fontsize=12)
plt.yscale('log') # Use Log scale so smaller businesses like Weed are still visible next to Cayo Perico
plt.xticks(rotation=45, ha='right')
plt.legend(fontsize=12)
plt.grid(axis='y', linestyle='--', alpha=0.4)

# Add text annotation for the winner
plt.annotate('36x ROI!\n(King of Efficiency)', 
             xy=(0, 144000000), xytext=(0.5, 120000000),
             arrowprops=dict(facecolor='black', shrink=0.05, width=2),
             fontsize=12, fontweight='bold', color='darkgreen')

plt.tight_layout()
plt.show()
<p>🏁 Project Conclusion This notebook successfully transformed raw, messy in-game statistics into a comprehensive Business Intelligence dashboard.</p> <p>Data Science Skills Demonstrated:</p> <p>Data Cleaning: Handling missing values and formatting strings to numeric types using Pandas. Feature Engineering: Creating custom financial metrics (Risk Scores, Payback Periods, Profit Margins) from raw data. Spatial Analysis: Mapping business locations onto a static coordinate grid using Matplotlib. Data Visualization: Creating automated pie charts, ROI bar charts, scatter plots, and ratio sketches. Business Acumen: Translating data outputs into actionable strategic insights. Thank you for reviewing the Los Santos Business Intelligence Portfolio.</p>
